text: Welcome to This is My Architecture. I'm Yongjin Cho from AWS. Today I have
Gwansu Lee from BNX Drainals. Hi Gwansu, thank you for joining. Thank you for having me.
So can you tell me about BNX and your architecture? Of course. BNX is an
entertainment-based IT company behind many global artists including BTS. We are
running two services, Weverse and Weverse Shop. Weverse is an official fan
community where global fans and artists interact and Weverse Shop is a global
merchandise shopping platform developed for our fans. As you know BTS has a huge
fandom. Because of that we have a special traffic pattern. When a member of BTS posted
a message we've experienced up to 9 million TPM instantly. It's like hundreds
times higher than usual. So we needed to come up with a solution to handle such a traffic.
Okay, well I think it's very interesting that a single message post can bring up
up to 9 million TPM. I think it will look really huge and then you should have come
up with very flexible and dynamic architecture. Yes. Can you tell me about
how I architect your platform? We call this event driven auto scaling architecture
which enables us to handle sudden and massive spikes in traffic. Of course we may provision instances
based on the peak traffic but it costs it could cost a lot and actually it is not a
proper way to utilize cloud. So we adopt this event driven auto scaling architecture.
Okay, so you architecture is event driven auto scaling architecture.
And I think that's why you have this lambda in the diagram, right? So can you tell me more
about your architecture? Of course. Actually we have two event cases but today I want to
focus more on unexpected events. When an artist create a new post, notifications are sent to
millions of users through PUSH server. At the same time it calls API endpoint which triggers a lambda function.
And this lambda in response to the massive traffic makes a change to the auto scaling group to meet the
number of instances required. Okay, so you're provisioning resources in the auto scaling group using lambda function, right?
Yes. Okay, and I think this is about scaling out but sometimes you have to go back to the original status, right?
So how do you do that? When the artist activity is done, then traffic returns to normal.
And this is the time when we have to scale in instances. So we trigger another lambda with AWS CloudWatchRue.
And it checks number of APIs and compare it to the normal state. And if the traffic is nearly normal,
then the auto scaling group returns to its own capacity.
Okay, this is great. So you're using CloudWatch to monitor the traffic patterns, right?
Yes.
And I just saw something very interesting here. So you're using spot instances in an auto scaling group.
Yeah.
And how do you use the spot instances?
Uh, despite all this effort, intermittent delays exist. It causes latency issues because the time of incoming
traffic received is way more faster than the healthy new instances created.
This is inevitable because EC2 itself needs to boot up and have the application started and warmed up.
Right.
So our team noticed that we have to provision instances in advance to handle all such event cases without any latency issue.
So we changed our architecture to utilize the spot instances.
And currently 70% of our instances are provisioned as a spot.
And thankfully we saved the cost up to 50% in EC2.
Okay. So it's also a very clever thing that you can use spot instances in auto scaling group, right?
So did you get any improvement in terms of latency with the spot instances?
Yes. Since we adopt spot instances, we've no longer experienced any delay by cold start.
Okay. So you completely solved the problem of using the spot instances, right?
Yes. And I have another thing, you know, that catches my eyes here. This is Goployer.
So I don't think this is an AWS service. Can you tell me about Goployer?
Yes. Goployer is an open source deployment tool that uses AWS auto scaling.
Our team made it to automate deployment process.
It runs based on the configurations in the manifest file so that we can implement deployment as code.
So, actually, Goployer supports most of auto scaling group features.
So anyone who is using auto scaling group can use our open source.
Okay. So basically this Goployer dynamically provisions auto scaling groups, right?
Yes.
Oh, this is great. I think this can be a superstar in the CICD world.
Yes.
So, thank you very much for the current architecture.
And I just want to ask you, you know, what's your future direction of this architecture?
Absolutely. We're still in search for the best way to deal with sudden and massive spike challenge.
So, we are considering now, we are considering EKS Fargate for EKS.
Okay.
EKS alone requires more node if I want to run more pod.
But with the Fargate, we don't need to provision more nodes for additional pods.
So, we expect that this could help us operate our services more reliable and cost efficient.
Okay. So, thank you very much for the explanation.
And I think you have a plan to make your life easier with AWS managing services, right?
Yes.
Okay.
So, today we introduced highly flexible architecture by BNX to handle massive peak traffic.
Thank you for joining us, Kwonsoo.
Thank you.
Thank you.
